metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify which design the `transition_close.py` `_completion_precheck` update should use: either (a) replace the `bug_close_reason_ok(reason)` call with a `close_class` enum validation, or (b) skip the pre-check entirely when `close_class` is supplied — pick one and state it in the plan.
evidence: 'update it to accept a valid `close_class` (or skip the reason pre-check when `close_class` is supplied)'

Two distinct behaviors: (a) keep the pre-check but validate close_class instead of reason; (b) bypass the pre-check entirely when close_class is present — different code paths with different safety properties.
findings.finding: The transition_close.py guard-update description offers two alternative designs without choosing one: 'accept a valid close_class (or skip the reason pre-check when close_class is supplied)'. An executor must pick one, which is a real design choice deferred to implementation.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py` bullet
scenarios: Executor implements option (b) and silently drops a pre-check that was protecting an invariant; or implements (a) and duplicates validation that txn.py already handles.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the parenthetical 'or' with a single decision, e.g.: 'replace the `bug_close_reason_ok(reason)` call with a `close_class_valid(close_class)` check using the same enum — do NOT skip the guard, just swap its predicate.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the fate of `--reason` in the plan: state whether it is (a) removed entirely, (b) kept as a no-op for backward compatibility, or (c) kept but mutual-exclusive with `--class`, and update the Files section to match the Approach's 'replacing' intent.
evidence: Files: 'parse a new `--class <value>` flag in `_parse_flags` (alongside `--reason`)'
Approach: 'replacing today's validated-then-discarded free-text `--reason`'
'--reason' fate not stated: kept for compat? error if supplied? silently ignored?
findings.finding: The Files section says parse `--class` 'alongside `--reason`' in transition.py, but the Approach section says `--class` is 'replacing today's validated-then-discarded free-text `--reason`'. It is unclear whether `--reason` is retained (backward-compat), deprecated, or removed.
findings.location: Files section (`transition.py` bullet) vs. Approach section
scenarios: An executor who reads 'alongside' keeps --reason; one who reads 'replacing' removes it. If kept, existing scripts that pass --reason silently succeed but close_class is now also required, causing a confusing dual-requirement CLI.
findings.suggested_fix: If intent is removal, change the Files bullet to: 'remove `--reason` parsing and add `--class <value>` in `_parse_flags`; callers still passing `--reason` receive an unknown-flag error.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC2's assertion to also verify the error output contains the allowed values, e.g.: `stderr output contains at least one of the enum value names (e.g. 'regression') — CLI test asserts this via string match on captured stderr`.
evidence: AC2: 'exits nonzero with a message naming the allowed values — CLI test asserts exit != 0'
The assertion 'exit != 0' does not verify that the message lists the enum values — a blank or misleading error satisfies the AC as written.
Approach: 'omitting it exits nonzero with a message listing the allowed values' — this UX claim is never measured.
findings.finding: AC2 states the error message should 'name the allowed values', but the actual test assertion only checks exit != 0 — message content is described but not measured, leaving the error-message quality claim unverified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2; Tests section
scenarios: Implementation emits a generic 'missing argument' error without listing values; AC2 still passes; the discoverability requirement silently goes unmet.
findings.suggested_fix: AC2: 'closing a bug with NO `--class` exits nonzero AND stderr contains the string `regression` (or the full list) — CLI test asserts exit != 0 AND `result.stderr` includes at least one enum value name.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC2/AC3) that verifies the CLI help text (`rebar transition --help` or `transition.txt`) lists the allowed `--class` values, covering the documented deliverable.
evidence: Files lists: 'src/rebar/_cli/help/transition.txt, docs/ticket-model.md, docs/user-guide.md — document the enum'
No AC mentions verifying documentation content, enum vocabulary listing in help text, or man-page accuracy — ABSENCE of coverage
findings.finding: Documentation files listed as deliverables (`transition.txt`, `docs/ticket-model.md`, `docs/user-guide.md`) have no covering acceptance criterion — they are orphan deliverables.
findings.location: Files section (transition.txt, docs/ticket-model.md, docs/user-guide.md) vs Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: Implementer skips docs updates and all four ACs still pass; the deliverable silently drops.
A reviewer cannot verify doc completeness against a criterion.
findings.suggested_fix: AC5: `rebar transition --help` (or the help text for the transition command) names all allowed `--class` values including `undetermined` — verified by grepping the output or the help file content.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC specifying the observable behavior when `--reason` is passed after this change (either rejected with nonzero exit, or explicitly documented as silently ignored), verifying the removal half of the replacement migration.
evidence: Approach: 'replacing today's validated-then-discarded free-text `--reason`'
Files (txn.py): 'replacing `bug_close_reason_ok` at the transition_core guard'
No AC reads: 'passing --reason (old flag) is rejected / ignored / no longer accepted' — ABSENCE
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion verifies that the old `--reason` flag is removed or rejected — the plan describes a replacement migration but only ACs covering the new `--class` behavior exist, leaving the removal half unverified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria vs Approach ('replacing today's validated-then-discarded free-text --reason')
scenarios: Implementer adds `--class` alongside `--reason` instead of replacing it; all four ACs pass but the described replacement is not enacted.
Old automation still passes `--reason`; whether it silently succeeds or breaks is unspecified and untested.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state what happens to callers passing `--reason` after this change (rejected or ignored), and either provide an expand-contract sequence (accept both flags temporarily) or explicitly justify the hard cutover with a note that `--reason` was never persisted so breakage is limited to invocation syntax.
evidence: Approach: 'back-out = stop requiring/writing it' — refers only to close_class
Approach: '--class is REQUIRED on bug close (mirrors the old required --reason)' — implies --reason is removed
No expand-contract or deprecation period described for --reason callers — ABSENCE of rollback for the removed interface
findings.finding: Callers currently using the required `--reason` flag will break after this change with no expand-contract sequence, deprecation window, or rollback path for that interface — the stated rollback ('back-out = stop requiring/writing [close_class]') only addresses the new flag and does not restore `--reason` for existing callers.
findings.location: Approach section ('back-out = stop requiring/writing it') vs implied breaking change to --reason callers
scenarios: A CI script or wrapper that calls `rebar transition <bug> in_progress closed --reason <text>` breaks immediately on deploy with no rollback path to the old interface.
If the change must be rolled back, implementers must restore --reason handling explicitly — the plan provides no instructions for this.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to Approach: 'Existing callers passing `--reason` will receive a nonzero exit with a message directing them to `--class`; no transition period is provided because `--reason` was never persisted and its only effect was validation. To roll back: revert txn.py and transition_close.py guard sites; no data migration needed because close_class is optional in the schema.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly confirm whether `--reason` is removed or kept alongside `--class`, and ensure all help text / docs (`transition.txt`, `docs/user-guide.md`, `docs/ticket-model.md`) purge or update every `--reason` reference accordingly.
evidence: "parse a new `--class <value>` flag in `_parse_flags` (alongside `--reason`)"
"replacing today's validated-then-discarded free-text `--reason`"
Plan lists updates to `transition.txt` and markdown docs but does not specify removal of `--reason` documentation
findings.finding: The Files section says `--class` is parsed 'alongside `--reason`' but the Approach says `--reason` is being replaced; if `--reason` is removed from the CLI, existing references to it in `transition.txt`, `docs/user-guide.md`, and any other help text become stale — the plan does not explicitly call out purging those references.
findings.location: Files section ('parse a new `--class <value>` flag in `_parse_flags` (alongside `--reason`)') vs. Approach ('replacing today's validated-then-discarded free-text `--reason`')
scenarios: A user reads the updated help text and sees both `--reason` and `--class` described, or sees `--reason` still documented after it has been removed from the CLI, causing confusion or incorrect usage.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a bullet to the Files section: 'Remove all `--reason` references from `transition.txt`, `docs/user-guide.md`, and any other help/doc files that describe the old flag.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Designate a single source-of-truth for the allowed `close_class` values (e.g., a `CLOSE_CLASSES` constant in a shared module or the JSON schema `enum` array imported by validators) so that adding or removing a class requires changing exactly one location.
evidence: "Closed MECE vocabulary with an escape + hygiene values: `regression`, `plan_defect`, `env_integration`, `flaky`, `preexisting`, `not_a_bug`, `duplicate`, `escalated`, `undetermined`"
Plan does not name a canonical constant/enum definition that both validation sites and the schema import
findings.finding: The nine enum values (`regression`, `plan_defect`, `env_integration`, `flaky`, `preexisting`, `not_a_bug`, `duplicate`, `escalated`, `undetermined`) are stated in the plan prose but no single authoritative source-of-truth location is designated; validation logic in `transition.py`, `txn.py`, and the JSON schema will each need to be updated when the vocabulary evolves, creating a multi-site maintenance burden with no central registry.
findings.location: Approach section (vocabulary list); Files: `transition.py`, `txn.py`, `ticket_state.schema.json`
scenarios: A maintainer adds a new class value, updates `txn.py` but forgets `transition.py` (or vice versa), causing inconsistent rejection messages; or schema and code drift apart silently.
findings.suggested_fix: Define `CLOSE_CLASSES: frozenset[str]` in a shared constants module (e.g., `src/rebar/_commands/constants.py`) and import it in both `transition.py` and `txn.py`; derive the schema `enum` array from the same source via a generation step or keep them in sync with a test assertion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or an AGENTS.md section) capturing the rationale for the closed enum, the escape value (`undetermined`), the `escalated`-branch mapping, and the two-guard architecture — so future contributors can evolve the vocabulary with intent.
evidence: Files lists `docs/ticket-model.md` and `docs/user-guide.md` but no ADR or AGENTS.md
"The guard lives at two sites … both updated" — architectural insight with no design-doc home
No mention of ADR or design rationale document anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: Replacing the free-text `--reason` with a bounded, folded enum is a non-trivial data-model decision (it changes the ticket's reduced state schema and the bug-close semantics permanently), but the plan names no ADR or AGENTS.md update to capture the rationale, vocabulary choices, and escape-valve design — `docs/ticket-model.md` is a user-facing reference, not a design-rationale document.
findings.location: Files section (doc updates listed); Approach section
scenarios: A future contributor wonders why the vocabulary is closed, why `escalated` maps to the old Escalated branch, or why `undetermined` exists as an escape value — there is no design document to consult, leading to ad-hoc vocabulary additions that erode the original intent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the behavior when `--class` is supplied for a non-bug ticket close: either reject with a clear message (e.g., '`--class` is only valid for bug tickets') or document that it is silently ignored — and add an AC or test case covering this path.
evidence: Plan states '`--class` is REQUIRED on bug close' but never addresses what happens when the flag is passed on a non-bug close
ACs only test bug-ticket scenarios; no AC covers supplying `--class` on a non-bug ticket type
findings.finding: The plan does not specify behavior when `--class` is supplied for a non-bug ticket close (e.g., a task or feature close). It is unspecified whether the flag is silently ignored, produces a warning, or is rejected — leaving an ambiguous and potentially confusing UX path.
findings.location: Files: transition.py, txn.py; Approach section; Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: User closes a task ticket while accidentally passing `--class regression` (e.g., from shell history); flag is silently dropped, giving no feedback and potentially masking a workflow mistake
CI script generically passes `--class` for all closes; non-bug closes silently succeed or fail unexpectedly
findings.suggested_fix: Reject `--class` on non-bug closes with a nonzero exit and a message like '`--class` is only meaningful for bug tickets; this ticket is type <type>'. Add one AC: 'closing a non-bug ticket with `--class regression` exits nonzero with an explanatory message'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least a debug/info log line in `_fold_close_class` (and/or in `txn.py` at write time) so that the presence or absence of `close_class` in the fold is traceable, enabling post-hoc debugging if classification metrics show unexpected gaps.
evidence: Approach describes the fold mechanic in detail but contains no mention of logging, metrics, or alerting for the new write or fold

CRITICAL callout for _processors.py notes that omitting the fold means 'close_class never surfaces into reduced state and rebar show cannot see it' — but no instrumentation is specified to detect this failure mode in production
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new persisted field (`close_class`) via a write in `txn.py` and a fold in `_processors.py`, but specifies no logging, metric, or trace at these new write/fold points — so operators have no signal if `close_class` fails to appear in reduced state (e.g., due to a missed fold branch).
findings.location: src/rebar/_commands/txn.py; src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py — Approach section ('_fold_close_class helper')
scenarios: A code path regression causes the fork-winner branch of process_status to skip _fold_close_class; close_class silently disappears from show output with no operator-visible signal
A schema migration edge case causes status_data to drop close_class before the fold; the value is lost silently
findings.suggested_fix: In `_fold_close_class`, add `logger.debug('folding close_class=%s for ticket %s', data["close_class"], state.get("id"))`. In `txn.py`, log at INFO when `close_class` is written to `status_data`. This is low-cost and closes the observability gap without requiring a full metrics pipeline.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (or add to the plan) how `close_class` is exposed in the MCP tool schema / dispatch path so that MCP callers can supply it, mirroring the CLI `--class` flag.
evidence: "parse a new `--class <value>` flag in `_parse_flags` (alongside `--reason`)"
"--class is REQUIRED on bug close (mirrors the old required `--reason`): omitting it exits nonzero"
Plan mentions no MCP-layer wiring for `close_class` in the tool schema or MCP dispatch path
findings.finding: The plan wires the new REQUIRED `--class` parameter exclusively through the CLI `_parse_flags` layer in `transition.py`, but does not address how the remote MCP server shape surfaces `close_class` as a callable parameter. If MCP tool dispatch bypasses `_parse_flags` and goes directly to `txn.py` or `transition_close.py`, MCP callers will have no way to supply `close_class`, and the required-field validation that replaces `bug_close_reason_ok` will reject every bug-close attempt over MCP.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/_commands/transition.py` — 'parse a new `--class <value>` flag in `_parse_flags`'; Approach: '`--class` is REQUIRED on bug close'
scenarios: Remote MCP server client shape: an agent calling the transition tool via MCP to close a bug ticket cannot pass `--class`; the `txn.py` guard rejects the call as missing the required field, making it impossible to close any bug via MCP after this change is deployed.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a plan step explicitly describing how the MCP tool definition for `transition` is updated to include `close_class` as a required parameter when `target_status=closed` and the ticket is a bug, so the required-field enforcement in `txn.py` is satisfiable from all three harness shapes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the `jq`-based AC assertion with a platform-agnostic alternative (e.g., a Python `json.loads` check inside the test file itself, or a `rebar show` subcommand that filters fields) so the AC is satisfiable on Windows CI.
evidence: "rebar show <bug> --output json | jq -e '.close_class==\"regression\"' matches (proving the fold reaches reduced state)"
jq is a Unix utility not bundled with Windows; Windows CI runners (GitHub Actions windows-latest, Azure Pipelines) require explicit jq installation steps
findings.finding: The Acceptance Criteria embed `jq` as the verification tool (`jq -e '.close_class=="regression"'`), but `jq` is not available by default on Windows. Because the plan specifies real subprocess tests (not mocked), the ACs as written cannot be satisfied on Windows CI or Windows developer workstations.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — first bullet: '`rebar show <bug> --output json | jq -e \'.close_class=="regression"\''`
scenarios: Windows platform (CI or developer workstation): running the AC test fails immediately because `jq` is not on PATH, so the fold-to-reduced-state proof can never be exercised on Windows, leaving the key correctness guarantee unverified on that platform.
findings.suggested_fix: In the test file, parse the JSON output with Python's built-in `json` module and assert `data['close_class'] == 'regression'` rather than piping through `jq`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit acknowledgment (and if needed, a migration note) in the plan for callers currently using `--reason` on bug closes, stating that `--reason` is being removed and `--class` is its replacement.
evidence: "replacing today's validated-then-discarded free-text `--reason`"
"--class is REQUIRED on bug close (mirrors the old required `--reason`)"
Plan contains no mention of deprecation period, backward-compatibility shim, or caller migration guidance for existing `--reason` usage
findings.finding: The plan replaces `--reason` with `--class` on bug closes but does not acknowledge existing callers (scripts, CI pipelines, MCP clients) that currently pass `--reason` when closing bugs. Since `--reason` was required and accepted, those callers will silently (or noisily) break after this change with no migration path described.
findings.location: Approach section: 'replacing today's validated-then-discarded free-text `--reason`'; Files: `transition.py` `_parse_flags`
scenarios: Any CI pipeline or script that currently calls `rebar transition <bug> in_progress closed --reason <text>` will receive a nonzero exit after this change, with no in-plan notice that this breakage was expected or how to migrate.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Breaking change' subsection to Approach naming `--reason` removal, and optionally add a transitional step that makes `--reason` emit a deprecation warning and map to `undetermined` for one release before removal.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a CLI test asserting that `rebar transition <bug> in_progress closed --class escalated` produces the same downstream Escalated-branch outcome as the previous free-text `--reason Escalated` path (verifying the changed/remapped behavior is preserved end-to-end).
evidence: Plan states: 'escalated preserves today's Escalated branch; the rest refine Fixed'
AC tests cover regression, undetermined, missing, and invalid — but no test for `--class escalated` producing the same downstream outcome as the old free-text Escalated path
This is a changed/remapped behavior under E5 checklist: 'Changed/deleted behaviors get corresponding modify/remove-test work'
findings.finding: The plan explicitly states that `escalated` preserves today's Escalated branch behavior (a remapped/changed behavior), but no test verifies that closing a bug with `--class escalated` triggers the Escalated branch end-to-end — the changed behavior gets no corresponding test.
findings.location: Tests section / Approach paragraph ('escalated preserves today's Escalated branch')
scenarios: A developer ships this change; the escalated branch silently breaks because the old free-text comparison is gone and the new enum path doesn't wire to the Escalated transition. Tests pass because no AC covers it.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: '`rebar transition <bug> in_progress closed --class escalated` exits 0 and the resulting ticket state reflects the Escalated branch (e.g. `rebar show <bug> --output json | jq -e ".close_class==\"escalated\""` and any branch-routing assertions that previously covered the free-text Escalated path).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test (or AC) that exercises the fork-winner branch of `process_status` to verify `close_class` is folded into reduced state when a concurrent/fork close is resolved, since the plan flags this as CRITICAL.
evidence: Plan states fold must be called in 'BOTH the fork-winner and normal-update branches — otherwise close_class never surfaces into reduced state'
Tests section only describes a single straightforward `rebar transition <bug> in_progress closed --class regression` flow — no concurrent/fork-winner scenario
The plan itself flags this as CRITICAL, implying the risk of missing it is high
findings.finding: The plan calls out the fork-winner branch in `reducer/_processors.py` as a CRITICAL dual-call site for `_fold_close_class`, but the test plan contains no scenario exercising this path — if the fork-winner branch is missing the fold, tests pass while the feature is silently broken for concurrent closes.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py`: 'called in BOTH the fork-winner and normal-update branches'
scenarios: Both code branches are required by the plan; only one is exercised by tests. A reviewer who adds the fold only to the normal-update branch ships broken behavior for concurrent closes, and the test suite stays green.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `close_ticket` (transition_close.py) and `transition_core` (txn.py) each need a `close_class` parameter added to their signatures and plumbing, so the value parsed by `_parse_flags` and extracted in `transition_compute` can reach `status_data`.
evidence: Plan: 'validate the enum for bug closes (replacing `bug_close_reason_ok` at the transition_core guard, ~line 221-232) and write `close_class` into `status_data`'
`transition_core` signature: `close_reason: str = ""` — no `close_class` param exists
`close_ticket` calls `txn.transition_core(..., close_reason=reason, ...)` at line 432 — `close_class` is not in the call
`close_ticket` signature: `reason: str, force_close: str` — no `close_class` param exists
Plan does NOT list `transition_close.py:close_ticket` as needing a signature change (only `_completion_precheck` is named)
findings.finding: The plan does not specify threading `close_class` through the `close_ticket` → `transition_core` call chain. `transition_core`'s current signature has `close_reason: str` (not `close_class`), and `close_ticket` calls it with `close_reason=reason` at line 432. The plan names `transition.py` and `txn.py` as the two files to touch, but `close_ticket` in `transition_close.py` is the intermediate layer that must also receive and forward `close_class`. Without specifying this plumbing, the `close_class` value parsed by `_parse_flags` cannot reach `status_data` in `transition_core`.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/_commands/txn.py` + `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py`; `close_ticket` call at transition_close.py line 425-435
scenarios: Developer updates `_parse_flags` to return `close_class` and updates `txn.transition_core`, but forgets to add `close_class` to `close_ticket`'s signature and the call from `transition_compute` → `close_ticket` — `close_class` is silently lost before reaching `transition_core`.
Developer updates `transition_compute` to extract `close_class` from `_parse_flags` output but has no guidance on how to forward it through `close_ticket` since the plan doesn't mention it.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `close_class: str = ""` to `close_ticket`'s signature and forward it to `transition_core`; add `close_class: str = ""` to `transition_core`'s signature and write it to `status_data` (conditioned on `target_status == 'closed' and ticket_type == 'bug'`). Document all four signature changes: `_parse_flags`, `transition_compute`, `close_ticket`, and `transition_core`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `_completion_precheck`'s signature must also gain a `close_class` parameter (or equivalent) so its bug-close pre-check can be updated to pass when a valid `close_class` is present, and document the threading path from `close_ticket` into `_completion_precheck`.
evidence: Plan: 'CRITICAL: the `_completion_precheck` guard at line ~121 also calls `txn.bug_close_reason_ok(reason)`; update it to accept a valid `close_class` (or skip the reason pre-check when `close_class` is supplied)'
`_completion_precheck` signature confirmed: `reason: str, force_close: str` — no `close_class` param
Call site at transition_close.py line 415-417: `_completion_precheck(ticket_id, ticket_type, repo_root_str, repo_root, reason=reason, force_close=force_close)` — no `close_class` forwarded
findings.finding: The plan asserts `_completion_precheck` should be updated 'to accept a valid `close_class` (or skip the reason pre-check when `close_class` is supplied)', but does not specify how `close_class` reaches `_completion_precheck`. Its current signature is `(ticket_id, ticket_type, cfg_root, repo_root, *, reason, force_close)` and the call in `close_ticket` at line 415-417 only passes `reason=reason, force_close=force_close`. Since `close_ticket`'s signature also lacks `close_class` (per finding above), the threading gap propagates here too.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py` — `_completion_precheck` at line ~121 and its call site at lines 415-417
scenarios: When `close_class` is supplied but `reason` is empty (since `--reason` is being replaced by `--class`), `_completion_precheck`'s bug-close check at line 121 (`if ticket_type == 'bug' and not txn.bug_close_reason_ok(reason)`) will always fire — blocking the close even when a valid `--class` was provided — unless `close_class` is threaded to `_completion_precheck` so it can distinguish 'reason absent because we're using class' from 'reason absent because user forgot'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether `close_class` needs to be seeded in `make_initial_state` (mirroring `claimed_session: None`) and add `reducer/_state.py` to the Files list if so, or explicitly justify the key-absent-until-closed design choice.
evidence: `make_initial_state` in `_state.py` seeds `claimed_session: None` (the exact analog the plan names) at line 23
`close_class` is not seeded in `make_initial_state`
Plan does not list `reducer/_state.py` in Files

`process_edit` skips fields not in `state` (`if field_name not in state: continue` at line 472) — but `_fold_close_class` writes directly to `state` so this restriction doesn't block it; however, key-absence vs None-presence affects consumers that use `state.get('close_class')` vs `'close_class' in state`
findings.finding: The plan says `make_initial_state` does NOT need updating, but the structural analogy it invokes (`_fold_claimed_session`) shows that `claimed_session` IS seeded in `make_initial_state` (line 23 of `_state.py`). If `close_class` is not seeded in `make_initial_state`, it will be absent from the initial state dict, which affects snapshot migration consistency (a post-feature SNAPSHOT that carries `close_class` is restored verbatim; a PRE-feature SNAPSHOT is not — but `make_initial_state` is the base that all pre-snapshot tickets replay onto, and not seeding means `close_class` is key-absent rather than `None` until a close event fires). The plan does not mention this and does not list `reducer/_state.py` in Files.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/reducer/_state.py` absent from file list; Approach section: 'Additive/optional schema field (readers tolerate absence)'
scenarios: A consumer checks `'close_class' in state` and finds it absent on tickets that were never closed (expected), but also finds it absent on tickets created after the feature but before any close — which could be confusing vs. `None`-seeded fields like `claimed_session`.

A SNAPSHOT written after the feature is deployed carries `close_class` in its compiled_state; a SNAPSHOT written before lacks it — the migration story (like the `claimed_session` 'NO active snapshot guard' note) is unaddressed in the plan.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"close_class": None` to `make_initial_state` in `_state.py` and add that file to the plan's Files list, with a note mirroring the `claimed_session` snapshot-guard rationale.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `close_class: str = ""` parameter to `rebar._lib_writes.transition()` (and thread it through to `transition_compute`), and document it; update `rebar._mcp_writes.transition_ticket` if bug closes via MCP should be supported.
evidence: plan lists only: transition.py, txn.py, transition_close.py, reducer/_processors.py, ticket_state.schema.json, help/transition.txt, docs/ — _lib_writes.py is absent

`result = _transition.transition_compute(resolved, current_status, target_status, force=force, reason=reason, force_close=force_close or '', repo_root=repo_root)` at _lib_writes.py:376 — no `close_class` forwarding
`rebar.transition` is re-exported in src/rebar/__init__.py:109 as the canonical public API
findings.finding: `rebar._lib_writes.transition()` (src/rebar/_lib_writes.py:329) is an unenumerated consumer of the close path that passes `reason=` to `transition_compute`. It is part of the public Python API (`rebar.transition`) re-exported from `rebar.__init__`. The plan never mentions adding a `close_class` parameter to this function, yet any code calling `rebar.transition(bug_id, 'in_progress', 'closed', ...)` without `close_class` will hit the new REQUIRED validation and exit nonzero. The library API will break for bug closes.
findings.location: src/rebar/_lib_writes.py:329 (`def transition`) — unenumerated consumer
scenarios: Any Python caller (workflow, test, integration) using `rebar.transition(bug_id, 'in_progress', 'closed')` gets exit nonzero silently because `close_class` is REQUIRED but the library signature never added it.
The MCP layer calls `rebar.transition(ticket_id, current_status, target_status)` (no `close_class`) so closing a bug via MCP is permanently broken.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `close_class: str = ""` to `_lib_writes.transition()` signature, forward it to `transition_compute(... close_class=close_class ...)`. In `_mcp_writes.py`, either add the param to the MCP tool or document that bug closes are CLI-only.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py` as an edit target: either pass a sentinel `close_class` (e.g. `undetermined`) when importing a closed bug ticket, or make `close_class` optional/skippable when `force=True`.
evidence: `transition_compute(local, 'open', 'closed', force=True, repo_root=rr)` called for all closeable tickets in import, no `close_class` arg
import_ndjson.py is absent from the plan's Files list
The plan states `--class` is REQUIRED on bug close: 'omitting it exits nonzero'
findings.finding: `src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py` calls `transition_compute(local, 'open', 'closed', force=True, repo_root=rr)` (line 356) for every ticket with `status='closed'` during import, including bug tickets. With `--class` REQUIRED for bug closes, this import path will fail for all imported bugs that lack `close_class` in their source NDJSON record. The plan does not enumerate `import_ndjson.py` as a consumer to update.
findings.location: src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py:356 — unenumerated consumer
scenarios: Running `rebar import` on an NDJSON export that contains closed bugs will fail for every bug ticket in the import set, because the close path now requires a `close_class` value.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/reducer/_state.py` to the edit target list and seed `'close_class': None` in `make_initial_state()`, exactly mirroring the `claimed_session` / `claim_harness` / `claim_remote_session` entries.
evidence: `make_initial_state` seeds `claimed_session: None`, `claim_harness: None`, `claim_remote_session: None` — the exact parallel fields; `close_class` is absent
Plan says `_fold_close_class` mirrors `_fold_claimed_session` but does not list `_state.py` as a file to edit
plan's Files section: `src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py` is listed but `src/rebar/reducer/_state.py` is not
findings.finding: `make_initial_state()` in `src/rebar/reducer/_state.py` seeds all folded fields that `_fold_claimed_session` writes (`claimed_session`, `claim_harness`, `claim_remote_session`). The plan introduces `_fold_close_class` as an exact parallel, but does NOT list `_state.py` as an edit target. Without seeding `close_class: None` in `make_initial_state`, pre-existing tickets (without a STATUS-closed event carrying `close_class`) will have the key absent rather than `None`, creating an inconsistent state shape that breaks schema consumers and the SNAPSHOT round-trip.
findings.location: src/rebar/reducer/_state.py — unenumerated edit target
scenarios: A ticket that was never closed will lack `close_class` in its state dict while a newly-closed bug has it, creating divergent key presence across tickets — callers doing `state.get('close_class')` will work, but `state['close_class']` will raise KeyError on pre-existing open tickets.
Compaction (SNAPSHOT) written before the change and replayed after will miss the key entirely, unlike `claimed_session` which is seeded and therefore always present.
findings.suggested_fix: In `make_initial_state()`, add `"close_class": None,` alongside the other STATUS-folded fields.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the `_USAGE` inline string in `src/rebar/_commands/transition.py` (lines 31-52) to the edit-target list and update it to document `--class` instead of (or alongside) `--reason`.
evidence: _USAGE at transition.py:40-41: `'--reason=<text>          Required when closing bug tickets. Must start with \'Fixed:\' or \'Escalated to user:\'.'`
plan's Files section lists `src/rebar/_cli/help/transition.txt` for documentation but not the inline `_USAGE` string in transition.py
plan's Approach says `_parse_flags` will parse `--class` but the inline usage string still documents `--reason` as the required param
findings.finding: The `_USAGE` inline string in `src/rebar/_commands/transition.py` (lines 31-52) explicitly documents `--reason` as required for bug closes (`'--reason=<text>  Required when closing bug tickets.'`). The plan lists `_cli/help/transition.txt` for documentation updates but does NOT call out the `_USAGE` string embedded in `transition.py` itself. If only `transition.txt` is updated, the `--reason` reference stays in the inline usage string and users see contradictory guidance.
findings.location: src/rebar/_commands/transition.py:31-52 (`_USAGE` string) — unenumerated edit target
scenarios: A user running `rebar transition --help` (or triggering the usage error path) sees the old `--reason` documentation even after the feature ships, causing confusion about which flag to use.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `close_ticket()` in `transition_close.py` to the list of files requiring a signature change (`close_class: str = ""` parameter), and thread it through to both `_completion_precheck` and `txn.transition_core` calls within that function.
evidence: `close_ticket(... *, reason: str, force_close: str)` at transition_close.py:353 — currently has no `close_class` parameter
`transition_compute` calls `close_ticket(... reason=reason, force_close=force_close)` at transition.py:265-274 — the plan must also pass `close_class` through here

`close_ticket` calls `_completion_precheck(... reason=reason, ...)` at transition_close.py:415-416 and `txn.transition_core(... close_reason=reason, ...)` at transition_close.py:425-434 — both need `close_class` routed through them

Plan says 'Thread `close_class` through the close path' under transition_close.py but marks it as updating only the `_completion_precheck` guard — omitting the `close_ticket` function signature itself
findings.finding: `close_ticket()` in `transition_close.py` is an unlistd intermediate function that must have its signature extended to accept and thread `close_class`, but the plan's Files section does not mention it.
findings.location: Files / `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py`
scenarios: Implementer updates `_completion_precheck` and `transition_core` for `close_class` but doesn't change `close_ticket`'s signature, so `close_class` is never forwarded from `transition_compute` into either guard — the enum is parsed at the CLI layer but silently dropped before reaching validation or persistence.
findings.suggested_fix: In `transition_close.py`, update `close_ticket(... *, reason, force_close)` → `close_ticket(... *, reason, force_close, close_class="")`, then pass `close_class=close_class` to both `_completion_precheck(...)` and `txn.transition_core(...)`. Update the call site in `transition_compute` to pass `close_class=close_class`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `"close_class": None` to `make_initial_state()` in `src/rebar/reducer/_state.py` to match the pattern of `claimed_session` and ensure `close_class` is key-present (null) in initial and pre-feature ticket state.
evidence: `make_initial_state()` in `reducer/_state.py` explicitly seeds `claimed_session: None`, `claim_harness: None`, `claim_remote_session: None` — the fields `_fold_claimed_session` writes
`process_edit` at `_processors.py:472`: `if field_name not in state: continue` — any field absent from `make_initial_state` is silently dropped by EDIT events
Plan says `_fold_close_class` 'mirrors `_fold_claimed_session`' but does not mention updating `make_initial_state` in the Files section or Approach
Schema says field is 'optional', but reducer state shape consistency requires a known default
findings.finding: The plan does not call out seeding `close_class` in `make_initial_state()`, breaking the exact parallel to `_fold_claimed_session` (which IS seeded) and causing `process_edit` to silently ignore any edit-path write of the field on pre-feature tickets.
findings.location: Approach / `src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py` (`_fold_close_class` helper)
scenarios: A SNAPSHOT from before this feature is replayed; `close_class` is absent from compiled_state; the fold works correctly post-close but the initial state has no key, causing `rebar show` to omit the field entirely for non-closed tickets — inconsistent with the 'additive/optional' shape contract.
A future EDIT event that attempts to clear `close_class` is silently ignored because the key was never seeded.
findings.suggested_fix: In `reducer/_state.py`, add `"close_class": None` to the `make_initial_state()` dict, adjacent to the other close-path fields.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly call out preserving the `from_idea` bypass (`current_status == 'idea'` exemption) when replacing the `bug_close_reason_ok` guard with the `close_class` enum check in `txn.transition_core`.
evidence: `txn.py:218-232`: `from_idea = current_status == 'idea'` guard explicitly bypasses the `bug_close_reason_ok` check for idea→closed transitions with the comment 'an undesigned idea has nothing built to verify or attest'
Plan says 'validate the enum for bug closes (replacing `bug_close_reason_ok` at the transition_core guard)' without mentioning preserving the `from_idea` exemption
The analogous bypass also exists in `transition_close.py:411`: `if target_status == 'closed' and current_status != 'idea':` skips `_completion_precheck`
findings.finding: The plan does not explicitly preserve the `from_idea` bypass in `txn.transition_core` for the new `--class` requirement, risking that `idea -> closed` transitions on bug tickets incorrectly require `--class`.
findings.location: `src/rebar/_commands/txn.py` — transition_core guard (~line 221)
scenarios: A user closes a bug idea (`rebar transition <bug-idea> idea closed`) — currently works without `--reason`. After the change, if `from_idea` bypass is not replicated for the `--class` guard, this command exits nonzero with 'must provide --class', breaking a currently-valid workflow.
findings.suggested_fix: In the `txn.py` guard section, keep the `from_idea = current_status == 'idea'` variable and use it in the new guard: `if target_status == 'closed' and ticket_type == 'bug' and not from_idea: <validate close_class>`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend the CLI test) asserting that `rebar transition --help` output no longer lists `--reason` as required on a bug close (or equivalently, that a bug close with `--class` and WITHOUT `--reason` exits 0), proving --reason is replaced rather than supplemented.
evidence: Parent SC: 'Bug-close friction stays flat (operationalized): rebar transition <bug> in_progress closed requires exactly one bug-specific argument both before and after this epic … The required-argument count for a bug close does not increase from 1 to 2; verified by diffing rebar transition --help against the pre-epic baseline'
Parent AC: 'Bug-close friction stays flat: … AND a rebar transition --help diff shows no net increase in required bug-close arguments.'
Leaf ACs cover: persist+surface, missing --class exits nonzero, invalid class rejected, undetermined accepted — none assert that --reason is dropped or that net required args stays at 1.
Leaf Approach says '--class is REQUIRED on bug close (mirrors the old required --reason)' but does not specify --reason is removed, and no AC enforces this.
findings.finding: The leaf omits a test/AC verifying that --reason is *removed* (not merely that --class is added), leaving the parent's 'friction stays flat' success criterion — 'a rebar transition --help diff shows no net increase in required bug-close arguments' — without a covering check in this leaf.
findings.location: Leaf Acceptance Criteria (all four ACs) vs. Parent Success Criteria ('Bug-close friction stays flat') and Parent AC ('rebar transition --help diff shows no net increase in required bug-close arguments')
scenarios: An implementer adds --class as a NEW required flag while leaving --reason also required, satisfying all four leaf ACs but failing the parent's friction-flat criterion — the leaf would pass its own test suite while the parent AC is violated.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: '`rebar transition <bug> in_progress closed --class regression` (with no `--reason` flag) exits 0 — confirming --reason is dropped, not added alongside --class; AND `rebar transition --help` diff shows no net increase in required bug-close arguments versus the pre-epic baseline.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit assertion in the Approach section about post-rollback schema state: state that (a) STATUS events written with `close_class` are immutable and field presence in SNAPSHOT compiled_state persists through rollback (benign forward-compat), (b) this compacted-vs-non-compacted divergence is intentional/acceptable, OR add a note that rollback requires a compensating-forward step (e.g., fsck rebuild-snapshot to strip `close_class` from compacted state).
evidence: Plan rollback statement: 'back-out = stop requiring/writing it'

process_snapshot in _processors.py lines 644-647: 'for key, value in compiled_state.items(): state[key] = value' — restores ALL compiled_state keys unconditionally, including close_class written before rollback

After rollback, _fold_close_class is removed from _processors.py, so raw-event-only (non-compacted) tickets lose the projection; SNAPSHOT-compacted tickets retain it via process_snapshot verbatim restore
T11 overlay requirement: 'the plan must assert on the schema STATE AFTER rollback, not merely that rollback exits 0'
findings.finding: The plan's rollback description ('stop requiring/writing it') does not assert the post-rollback schema STATE. Specifically, STATUS events already written with `close_class` in `data` are immutable; after rollback, non-compacted tickets will NOT surface `close_class` in reduced state (because `_fold_close_class` is removed), but SNAPSHOT-compacted tickets WILL continue to surface it (because `process_snapshot` restores all `compiled_state` keys verbatim). This compacted-vs-non-compacted divergence is not acknowledged, and the plan makes no assertion about whether this inconsistency is acceptable or how to resolve it.
findings.location: ## Approach — 'Additive/optional schema field (readers tolerate absence); back-out = stop requiring/writing it.'
scenarios: A bug is closed with --class regression, then compact_ticket runs (compact-on-close fires per transition_close.py line 61). After rollback, rebar show on that ticket still surfaces close_class=regression (via SNAPSHOT). A non-compacted ticket closed the same way does NOT surface it. Two identically-treated bugs appear differently after rollback.
A metrics query post-rollback that checks for absence of close_class to detect 'old-style' closes will get false negatives for compacted tickets.

findings.finding: The plan does not specify a process_snapshot migration guard for close_class, unlike every other additive state field (managed_refs, attestations, keyring, creation_channel all have explicit migration guards in process_snapshot). A pre-feature SNAPSHOT lacking close_class will restore verbatim (key-absent), while a post-feature SNAPSHOT carries it. The plan's _processors.py bullet only mentions _fold_close_class; it does not address the snapshot migration path.
findings.location: ## Files (reducer/_processors.py bullet) + src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:643-707

Consistency: every other new state field has this guard; omitting it creates a subtle inconsistency reviewers will flag.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly enumerate the four signature changes needed to thread close_class end-to-end: (1) `_parse_flags` return tuple gains `close_class`, (2) `transition_compute` gains `close_class: str = ""` parameter and passes it to `close_ticket`, (3) `close_ticket` gains `close_class: str = ""` and passes it to `txn.transition_core`, (4) `transition_core` gains `close_class: str = ""` and writes it to `status_data` on the bug-close path.
evidence: Plan transition.py bullet: 'parse a new --class <value> flag in _parse_flags (alongside --reason)' — no mention of return-tuple signature change
Plan txn.py bullet: 'write close_class into status_data' — but transition_core's signature must also gain a close_class parameter

transition_close.py line 353: 'close_ticket(..., *, reason: str, force_close: str)' — must add close_class parameter
txn.py line 159: 'transition_core(..., *, ..., close_reason: str = "", force_close_reason: str = "", ...)' — must add close_class parameter
findings.finding: The plan does not enumerate the call-chain signature changes needed to thread close_class from _parse_flags through transition_compute, close_ticket, and into txn.transition_core where status_data is populated. Currently _parse_flags returns (reason, force, force_close); transition_compute accepts reason=; close_ticket accepts reason=; transition_core accepts close_reason=. The plan says 'Thread close_class through the close path' but lists no intermediate signatures, making the wiring implicit and leaving implementors to discover the full chain themselves.

A partial wiring (close_ticket receives it but transition_core doesn't write it to status_data) means the value is validated but never persisted.

findings.finding: The plan does not state whether `--class` is required or optional when closing a bug ticket. 'Replacing' the required `--reason` implies required, but 'additive/optional field' implies optional. No AC tests the omission case (close a bug without `--class`).
findings.location: Approach section — 'Additive/optional field (readers tolerate absence); back-out = stop requiring/writing it'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to Approach: '`--class` is required on bug close (mirrors the old `--reason` requirement); omitting it exits nonzero with a message listing allowed values.' Add a fourth AC: `rebar transition <bug> in_progress closed` (no `--class`) exits nonzero.

findings.finding: The plan does not specify the fallback behavior when `--class` is omitted on a bug close — whether it is required (non-zero exit), optional with a default (e.g. `undetermined`), or simply allowed to be absent — leaving the processing rule ambiguous for implementers and the AC incomplete.
findings.location: Approach section / Acceptance Criteria

findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether `--class` is required or optional when closing a bug. The schema marks `close_class` as optional, yet the plan says it 'replaces' the formerly required `--reason`, implying it may be mandatory. No AC covers the omitted-`--class` case, leaving the user-facing behavior undefined.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria / ## Approach ('additive/optional field') / src/rebar/_commands/txn.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: `rebar transition <bug> in_progress closed` (no --class) either exits nonzero with a message listing allowed values, OR exits 0 with `close_class` absent and document that omission is intentional. Pick one and encode it in the test.

findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether --class is required or optional for a bug close at the CLI level, and neither branch (omission-is-an-error OR omission-silently-defaults) is covered by a test, leaving the most common user mistake (closing a bug without --class) untested.
findings.location: ## Tests / ## Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Add one AC line: 'closing a bug without `--class` [succeeds, writing no `close_class` field | defaults to `undetermined` | is rejected with exit != 0 and a message listing --class]' — pick one, then add a CLI subprocess assertion for it.

findings.finding: The plan asserts `bug_close_reason_ok` is being 'replaced' and only lists `txn.py` for that change, but `bug_close_reason_ok` is called from a SECOND site in `transition_close._completion_precheck` (transition_close.py:121) that the plan does not address. If `--reason` is removed/relaxed for bug closes, that second guard will still reject closes lacking a `Fixed:`/`Escalated:` prefix, silently breaking the `--class` flow when the completion-verification gate is on.
findings.location: Files section / Approach section; src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py:121
findings.suggested_fix: In `_completion_precheck`, replace the `txn.bug_close_reason_ok(reason)` call with a check that a valid `close_class` value was supplied (parallel to what txn.py's transition_core does after the change). Alternatively, when `close_class` is set, skip the reason pre-check entirely in `_completion_precheck`.

findings.finding: bug_close_reason_ok has two call sites — txn.transition_core (line 228) AND _completion_precheck in transition_close.py (line 121) — but the plan only names txn.py for the validation replacement. If --class replaces --reason (making reason empty), _completion_precheck's existing check `if ticket_type == 'bug' and not txn.bug_close_reason_ok(reason)` will raise a CommandError blocking every completion-verified bug close, even with a valid --class value.
findings.location: Files section (src/rebar/_commands/txn.py); src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py line 121

findings.finding: The plan's replacement of `bug_close_reason_ok` is specified only for `txn.py`, but the same predicate is also called in `transition_close.py` (line 121, inside `_completion_precheck`); the plan does not say how that second call site is handled.
findings.location: Files list / src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py line 121 / Approach section

findings.finding: The plan describes replacing `--reason` (a migration), but no AC verifies the removal/deprecation side — only the replacement (`close_class` persistence) is tested. For a migration, criteria should verify BOTH sides.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section

findings.finding: The title and Approach say `--reason` is being 'replaced' by `--class`, but the file-level description for `transition.py` says the new flag is added 'alongside `--reason`', leaving it ambiguous whether `--reason` is removed, deprecated-but-kept, or silently ignored after this change. Callers currently passing `--reason` on bug closes are affected differently depending on which interpretation is correct, and no AC covers the fate of `--reason`.
findings.location: Files section — `transition.py` bullet: 'parse a new `--class <value>` flag in `_parse_flags` (alongside --reason)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Approach: 'The existing `--reason` flag will be removed (or deprecated with a warning) as part of this change; any caller passing `--reason` on a bug close will receive [error / deprecation warning].' Then add an AC: '`rebar transition <bug> in_progress closed --reason old_value` exits nonzero (or with a deprecation warning) after this change.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts that writing `close_class` into `status_data` means it 'survives' and will be readable via `rebar show <bug> --output json | jq -e '.close_class=="regression"'` (AC1). But the reducer's `process_status` in `_processors.py` does NOT automatically promote arbitrary `status_data` dict keys to the top-level ticket state: only `status`, `current_status`, `parent_status_uuid`, the session fields, and `last_reopened_at` are explicitly folded. `close_class` in the event's `data` dict will be stored on disk but will NOT appear in `reduce_ticket()` output, making AC1 impossible to pass without a reducer change.
findings.location: Approach section ('written into `status_data` so it survives') / AC1; src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:195-292
findings.suggested_fix: In `process_status` (or a new `_fold_close_class` helper), after a `* -> closed` edge is applied, do: `if data.get('status') == 'closed' and data.get('close_class'): state['close_class'] = data['close_class']`. Also add `close_class` to `ticket_state.schema.json` as the plan already notes.

findings.finding: `reducer/_processors.py` is missing from the edit-set: `process_status` must fold `close_class` from `status_data` into top-level ticket state (mirroring how `_fold_claimed_session` folds `session` into `claimed_session`) for the AC assertion `rebar show <bug> --output json | jq -e '.close_class=="regression"'` to pass.
findings.location: Files section / AC line 1
findings.suggested_fix: In `process_status` (non-fork and fork-winner branches), after the existing `_fold_claimed_session` call, add: `if data.get('status') == 'closed' and data.get('close_class'): state['close_class'] = data['close_class']`.

findings.finding: The plan never adds the fold step in the reducer's process_status (or a _fold_close_class helper) that promotes close_class from the raw STATUS event's data dict into the reduced ticket state. Without this step, close_class is durable in the event file but invisible to rebar show, making the primary AC assertion (`jq -e '.close_class=="regression"'` on show output) fail.
findings.location: Files section (missing: src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py); Acceptance Criteria line 1
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `_fold_close_class(state, data)` helper that sets `state['close_class'] = data.get('close_class')` when `data.get('status') == 'closed'`, and call it from `process_status` in both the fork-winner and normal-update branches (mirroring `_fold_claimed_session`). List `src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py` in Files.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
